Scenario.title: Water Curupira Pikabot Distribution in Energy Sector
Scenario.overview: Energy Complex The attackers leverage AI-driven reconnaissance, phishing, and script-based exploitation to infiltrate energy infrastructure, escalating privileges and disrupting SCADA systems to cause power outages.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Open Source Intelligence (OSINT), Command, Internet-facing assets, Application Log, Windows Registry, Domain Name Registrar, OS Process, Windows Service, Process, Network Traffic, File, Malware Artifact, Boot Sector
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Artificial Intelligence, Command Execution (Mechanism), Delivery Method, Application Log Content (Mechanism), Windows Registry Key Creation (Mechanism), Pass the Hash, Process Injection, Keylogging, Reg.exe Utility, Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), File Modification (Mechanism), Encrypted Channel, Modify System Partition

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attackers use AI tools to gather information about energy infrastructure and employee details from public sources, identifying potential targets and vulnerabilities.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: Basic scripting is employed to develop phishing templates and malicious payloads, enabling the creation of tailored attacks for subsequent deployment.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Phishing emails with malicious macro-enabled documents are sent to employees, delivering the Pikabot malware via email attachments.

stage.name: Execution
stage.description: The payload is triggered through compromised email clients, exploiting application log content to execute malicious code on the target system.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: Scheduled tasks are created in the Windows Registry to maintain persistent access to infected systems.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC-461: Web Services API Signature Forgery Leveraging Hash Function Extension Weakness
stage.description: Stolen credentials are leveraged using Pass the Hash techniques to escalate privileges and access higher-level system resources.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malicious activities are hidden within legitimate processes to evade detection by security mechanisms.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.description: A keylogger is deployed to steal login credentials from victim systems, facilitating further lateral movement.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Network sniffing tools like Reg.exe are used to map internal network topology and identify critical systems for targeted attacks.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: Lateral movement occurs via Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP), utilizing network traffic patterns to move undetected across the infrastructure.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: Operational data from control systems is extracted and exfiltrated, targeting critical infrastructure components.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Stolen data is transferred through encrypted tunnels to obscure the exfiltration activity from monitoring tools.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: The final stage involves modifying the boot sector of SCADA systems to disrupt operations and cause intentional power outages.

Scenario.summary: The Water Curupira Pikabot campaign demonstrates a multi-stage attack lifecycle targeting energy infrastructure, combining low-skill phishing with advanced persistence and privilege escalation techniques to achieve operational disruption.
